coredata.dc:title: Preparation of an ultra-sensitive electrochemical sensor for morphine measurement using the ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Akrami, Zakyeh
Sohouli, Esmail
coredata.dc:description: Morphine is one of the most important narcotic drugs used for relieving severe pain. However, excessive use of morphine can lead to respiratory depression, brain damage, and even death, thus caution is necessary in its use. Given the severe problems morphine can cause to humans, measuring it with a straightforward and rapid method is important. Therefore, a straightforward and cost-effective electrochemical sensor was prepared using a ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite as a modifier for the glassy carbon electrode. After investigating and analyzing the synthesized nanocomposite and modifying the glassy carbon electrode with cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry methods, morphine measurement was performed. The results indicated a low detection limit (3 nM) for morphine measurement in the concentration range of 0.01–75 μM. In addition to good repeatability and reproducibility, the prepared sensor based on ZnS/Mil-125 has excellent capability in real sample measurement and suitable selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Morphine
ZnS
Mil-125
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnS and its nanocomposite with Mil-125
To prepare the real samples for analysis
Performance of the prepared electrochemical sensor in measuring morphine
Investigation of the effect of pH on the electrooxidation of morphine
Investigation of scan rate in the oxidation of morphine
Investigation of the amount of nanocomposite in electrode modification and morphine measurement
Repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and selectivity of the electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM image of (a) ZnS, (b) Mil-125, (c,d) ZnS/Mil-125.
ED’s pattern and map of ZnS/Mil-125.
(a) CVs of voltamogramms of MO at scan rates different, (b) I vs υ1/2 and (c) V vs Log I.
CV of GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 in (a) absence and (presence), (c) CVs and (d) EIS Plot of GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 in probe solution.
(a) DPVs voltammograms of MO at different concentrations and (b) I vs different concentrations.
Dependence of (a) E and (b) I on pH.
Comparison of the various sensor developed for MO analysis.

head.ce:title: Preparation of an ultra-sensitive electrochemical sensor for morphine measurement using the ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Education, Farhangian University, P.O. Box 14665-889, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry Education, Farhangian University,

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry Education, Farhangian University, P.O. Box 14665-889, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Morphine is one of the most important narcotic drugs used for relieving severe pain. However, excessive use of morphine can lead to respiratory depression, brain damage, and even death, thus caution is necessary in its use. Given the severe problems morphine can cause to humans, measuring it with a straightforward and rapid method is important. Therefore, a straightforward and cost-effective electrochemical sensor was prepared using a ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite as a modifier for the glassy carbon electrode. After investigating and analyzing the synthesized nanocomposite and modifying the glassy carbon electrode with cyclic voltammetry and differential pulse voltammetry methods, morphine measurement was performed. The results indicated a low detection limit (3 nM) for morphine measurement in the concentration range of 0.01–75 μM. In addition to good repeatability and reproducibility, the prepared sensor based on ZnS/Mil-125 has excellent capability in real sample measurement and suitable selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Morphine (MO), recognized as a significant opioid drug, is well-known for its pain-relief properties in patients who have undergone surgery [1–3]. Numerous studies have substantiated its prompt pain-relieving effects in peripheral regions. Furthermore, MO is used to treat both long-standing and sudden pain, notably during childbirth and in cases of heart attack [4,5]. Nonetheless, the potential side effects from overuse, such as nausea, constipation, and lethargy, highlight the importance of meticulous control over its dosage and ongoing monitoring in biological samples [6,7]. Particularly, MO exhibits a high potential for addiction and is frequently misused [8,9]. It affects the brain’s reward center, which is accountable for the feeling of pleasure that comes from fundamental life activities such as eating. When abused, MO can be extremely harmful, affecting a variety of immune responses and leading to issues including disruptions in the central nervous system, increased breathing rates, and lowered blood pressure [10]. It is important to note that around 90 % of orally ingested MO is eliminated from the body through urine within a 24-hour period, and this includes 10 % that remains unchanged. Therefore, it is essential to monitor the concentrations of MO in the blood and urine for both medical and legal purposes [11]. Furthermore, the detection of MO in human blood is important in the context of forensic analysis and the study of drug movement and actions within the body (pharmacokinetics) [12]. Various techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography [13], fluorimetry [14], surface plasmon resonance [15], and electrochemical methods [16–19], have been employed for analytical purposes. Electrochemical techniques are highly regarded for their quick turnaround, precise selectivity, high sensitivity, straightforward usage, and cost-effectiveness. The wide-ranging applications, ease of handling, and affordability of electrochemical sensors further establish their dominance over alternative analytical approaches [20–23]. As a result, numerous scientists have implemented a variety of modified electrodes for the purpose of detecting MO through electrochemical means [24–26]. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE), known for its high electrical conductivity, impressive chemical stability, wide potential window, and low background current, stands out as an ideal candidate for crafting sensitive and precise electrochemical sensors. This has led to its selection for the development of a MO electrochemical sensor. Despite its merits, the electrode faces limitations due to surface fouling and the adsorption of reaction products, which can affect sensor performance [27–29]. To overcome these drawbacks, it’s essential to enhance the GCE with modifiers that boast electrocatalytic properties. Different modifiers such as single-walled carbon nanotubes [30], Graphene/Co3O4 [31], graphene oxide/Fe3O4@SiO2 [31], reduced graphene oxide [32], and graphene nanoribbon [33] have been utilized in the development of MO electrochemical sensors.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), recently identified as a unique type of nanoporous crystalline material, have gained significant interest due to their potential as effective modifiers. These materials are distinguished by their composite structure consisting of metallic ions or clusters interconnected by multifunctional organic ligands [34,35]. This last decade has seen a surge in interest towards MOFs due to their chemical adaptability and structural variety, resulting in their widespread use across several fields such as catalysis, gas storage and separation, molecular detection, medication delivery, bioimaging, electronic devices, solar energy conversion, and light-induced chemical reactions [36]. Some metal ions present in MOFs have demonstrated effective electrocatalytic capabilities, making them suitable for enhancing electrodes and developing efficient electrochemical sensors. Different MOFs, such as chromium metal−organic framework nanoparticles [Cr(III)-MOF-NPs] [37], Cu-Hemin metal-organic framework (CHM) [38], Zr-based MOF (ZIF-67) [39] and UiO-66-NH2 MOF [40], have been utilized to detect MO concentrations. Recently garnering attention, the MIL-125(Ti) MOF, characterized by its crystalline and porous nature, provides a vast surface area and ample redox sites, making it exceptionally suitable for electrochemical sensing and energy systems [41,42]. This efficiency in electron and mass transportation, alongside their compatibility with materials such as metal nanoparticles and carbon composites, considerably bolsters their catalytic performance, making them particularly valuable in fuel cell technologies [43]. Utilizing MIL-125(Ti) in electrochemical sensors enhances sensitivity and selectivity due to its vast surface and porous structure, improving analyte interaction and electron transfer. Its synthetic flexibility allows the addition of electroactive sites and easy electrolyte access, improving ion transport and boosting device performance [44–46]. Various electrochemical sensors have been prepared based on MIL-125. For example, a SPCE enhanced with an Au/NH2-MIL-125(Ti) composite was used in the electrochemical detection of gallic acid in green tea and urine samples [47]. Moreover, NH2-MIL-125(Ti) and its derivative, functionalized with emeraldine, served as chemical sensors for the effective detection of dopamine [48]. A photoelectrochemical sensing system was developed using an NH2-MIL-125(Ti)-TiO2 composite, designed for the sensitive and selective detection of oxtetracycline through molecular imprinting [49]. Molecularly imprinted polymers, functionalized with COOH and originating from MIL-125(Ti), were attached to TiO2 to facilitate the photoelectrochemical sensing of ofloxacin [50]. Furthermore, a state-of-the-art electrochemical sensor has been devised, employing a Cu3P@NH2-MIL-125(Ti) nanocomposite, to achieve highly effective electrocatalytic oxidation and precise detection of hydrazine [51]. Although MIL(125) is a popular choice for the measurement of several drugs, it has not been applied to the detection of MO. The addition of sulfides, particularly sulfide (ZnS), into MIL(125) is likely to boost the performance of the electrochemical sensor, thereby enhancing the electrode’s sensitivity to its maximum potential. ZnS, a notable semiconductor in the II-VI group with a wide band gap of 3.7 eV, stands out as a potential candidate owing to its superior biocompatibility, chemical stability, and catalytic efficiency [52].The outstanding thermal and chemical robustness of ZnS, combined with its ability to adopt a range of morphologies, has spurred widespread investigation into its use in diverse domains. Such applications span sensors, LEDs (light-emitting diodes), photocatalytic processes, energy storage solutions, flat-panel displays, electroluminescent devices, biocatalysis, laser technology, and infrared windows [53]. Electrodes modified with nanostructured ZnS stand out due to their expanded surface area, exceptional biocompatibility, enhanced catalytic performance, and robust chemical durability, showcasing notable features and suitability for use in biosensor applications [54]. Up until now, materials like L-cysteine [55], ganciclovir [56], streptomycin [57], glucose [58], and olanzapine [59] have been effectively identified using electrodes composed of ZnS or ZnS-based composites.

Given the benefits of the modifiers MIL(125) and ZnS nanoparticles, an electrochemical sensor incorporating these two materials was developed for MO detection in biological samples, utilizing SPCEs. The composite underwent extensive characterization through multiple analytical methods, such as, X-ray diffraction (XRD), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), elemental analysis, and Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The performance of the electrode was assessed using cyclic voltammetry, examining how temperature, scan rate, MO concentration, and pH influenced its function. Ultimately, differential pulse voltammetry was employed for the quantitative analysis of MO in actual biological matrices, such as plasma.

Terephthalic acid, tetra n-butyl titanate, dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol, ammonia, morphine, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), sodium sulfide, zinc acetate dehydrate, sodium hydroxide, potassium chloride, of analytical grade were purchased from shimimodern (Iran) companies and used in this work. A phosphate buffer was prepared by dissolving 0.1 M potassium dihydrogen phosphate and adjusting the pH with 0.1 M sodium hydroxide, and it was used as the electrolyte solution. A 1 mM morphine solution was also prepared by dissolving morphine in methanol and then diluting it with water. The electrochemical probe solution was prepared from 1 mM potassium hexacyanoferrate(III)/potassium hexacyanoferrate(II) trihydrate in 0.1 M potassium chloride. The μ-Autolab III from Switzerland was used for electrochemical testing with methods such as cyclic voltammetry, differential pulse voltammetry, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy using Nova 2.1 software. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were done under a frequency range of 1 kHz–100 mHz. The Mira3 TESCAN field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Czech Republic), transmission electron microscopy Philips EM208S 100KV (TEM, Nederland) was used to examine the morphology of the synthesized nanomaterials, and a BET model SA-9603 (Japan) was used to examine the surface area of the synthesized materials. All voltammetric experiments were conducted at ambient temperature. In the cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests, scan rates varied from 0.01 to 0.4 V/s, while in the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) tests, a step potential of 0.005 V, modulation amplitude of 0.025 V, modulation duration of 0.05 seconds, interval time of 0.05 seconds, and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed.

2.2 g of terephthalic acid was mixed with 2 mmol of tetra n-butyl titanate in a 50 mL container containing DMF and methanol in a 4:1 ratio and stirred at room temperature for 30 min before being transferred to an autoclave. The autoclave was then placed at 150 ℃ for 48 h. After the reaction, the obtained powder was washed with DMF and methanol. Subsequently, it was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnS and its nanocomposite with Mil-125
ce:section.ce:para: The coprecipitation method was used to synthesize ZnS. 5 mmol of zinc acetate dehydrate and EDTA as a capping ligand were dissolved in 50 mL of water, and the pH was adjusted to 7 using ammonia. In another beaker, 0.1 mol of sodium sulfide was dissolved in 50 mL of water. The contents of this beaker were added to the container containing zinc acetate under stirring at a temperature of 60 ℃, resulting in a white precipitate. After stirring at 60 ℃ for 6 h, the obtained powder was separated using centrifugation, washed with water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 24 h. The synthesis of ZnS/MIL-125 was also carried out in the same manner in the presence of 0.1 g of Mil-125.

The electrode is a GCE polished with alumina powder 5μm, then sonicated in a mixture of water and acetone for 2 min to remove surface impurities. The electrode is then placed in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution and cycled using a voltammetric method within the potential range of 1 to −1 V at a scan rate of 100 mV/s 10 consecutive scans to activate its surface. Subsequently, 5 mg of nano-composite ZnS/Mil-125 in the presence of 5 % Nafion in 5 mL of ethanol was dispersed, and 5 μL of it were placed on the electrode surface. The electrode was dried at 50 °C. The modification of the electrode with ZnS and Mil-125 was also performed using the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: To prepare the real samples for analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood serum samples obtained from a local medical diagnostic laboratory underwent pretreatment through centrifugation at 3500 rpm for 10 min. The resulting samples were then spiked with specific quantities of MO and subsequently diluted fivefold with buffer (pH 7.0) to ensure operation within the method's linear range. The standard addition method was employed to mitigate any potential matrix effects.

Fig. 1 presents the FE-SEM images of ZnS, Mil-125, and ZnS/Mil-125. The small spherical nanoparticles of ZnS are visible here, with the particle size for ZnS being approximately 10–20 nm. Additionally, the FE-SEM images for Mil-125 show a coinly (colony) morphology or tablet-like, which, when combined with ZnS to form a nanocomposite, the presence of spherical ZnS nanoparticles on their surface is evident. Also, the elemental EDS analysis for the ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite is presented in Fig. 2. The presence of elements Zn, S, Ti, C and O with percentages 25.28, 24.34, 4.43, 32.58 and 13.37 % indicates the successful synthesis of this nanocomposite. The TEM images of ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite are shown in Figure S1. The presence of spherical ZnS nanoparticles and Mil-125 is well-defined.

In Fig. 4, the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen gas adsorption/desorption technique plot for the synthesized samples is presented in Figure S2. The surface areas obtained from BET for samples ZnS, Mil-125, ZnS/Mil-125 are 81.32, 1201.3, and 1279.2 m2/g, respectively. Therefore, the synthesized materials possess good surface areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the prepared electrochemical sensor in measuring morphine
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3a displays the cyclic voltammograms (CV) for electrodes GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 immersed in a pH 7 phosphate buffer, spanning a potential range from −0.2–0.7 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. notably, no oxidative or reductive peaks are evident on these electrodes, suggesting that the materials used for modification lack electroactivity. Furthermore, the pristine electrode surface and the electrolyte are confirmed to be clean, as indicated by the absence of any peaks. In contrast, upon introducing 30 μM of MO into the electrolyte solution, a distinct oxidative peak emerges at 0.4 V on the unmodified electrode, attributable to morphine oxidation, with a peak current of 1.48 μA observed at GCE. Incorporating zinc sulfide nanoparticles onto the surface of the GCE enhances the oxidative current of morphine whit oxidative potential (0.36 V). Similarly, modification of the GCE with Mil-125 increases the oxidative current to 4.22 μA, with the peak remaining at 0.37 V. Combining ZnS and MIL-125 into a nanocomposite significantly boosts the oxidative current of morphine, maintaining the peak potential at 0.36 V, indicative of a synergistic effect between zinc sulfide and Mil-125 (Fig. 3b). An electrochemical probe solution further assessed the electrodes’ enhanced performance. Fig. 3a illustrates the cyclic voltammograms for GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 in this solution, with the potential difference between the oxidative and reductive peaks being 0.21, 0.17, 0.15 and 0.09 V, respectively (Fig. 3c). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy revealed that the charge transfer resistance, represented by the semicircle diameter, for GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125, is 581.4, 201.2, 276.2 and 58 Ω, respectively (Fig. 3d). Among these, the ZnS/MIL-125 nanocomposite-modified electrode exhibits superior performance, attributed to the complementary properties of ZnS and MIL-125. The Randles–Sevcik equation was used to calculate the surface areas of GCE, GCE/ZnS and GCE/ZnS/Mil-125, which were found to be 0.015, 0.024, 0.021 and 0.037 cm−2, respectively. The ZnS/MIL-125 nanocomposite-modified electrode boasts a larger surface area, with MIL-125’s unique morphology providing additional active sites for analyte oxidation. Zinc sulfide’s contribution to improving MIL-125’s electrical conductivity further augments the electrode’s performance. Consequently, the heightened oxidative current of the nanocomposite-modified electrode can be ascribed to its enhanced conductivity and greater surface area, which facilitate a more rapid electron transfer rate and superior performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the effect of pH on the electrooxidation of morphine
ce:section.ce:para: The investigation of the effect of pH on morphine at the surface of GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 was conducted within the pH range of 2–9.Notably, the oxidative current peaks at pH 7, as evidenced by the highest recorded current at GCE/ZnS/Mil-125. This peak signifies the selection of pH 7 as the optimal pH for the process. Additionally, Table 1 details the oxidative potential of morphine at varying pH levels within the electrolyte solution. A notable shift in oxidative potential accompanies the increase in pH, and the resulting potential-pH plot yields a slope of 63 (E = -0.637 pH + 0.824). This value aligns closely with the theoretical predictions of the Nernst equation, suggesting an equal number of electrons and protons are involved in the oxidation process of morphine. The electrochemical process morphine involves the loss of electrons, which may lead to the formation of an oxidized species. The hydroxyl groups (-OH) present in the morphine structure can be involved in the oxidation process, potentially forming carbonyl groups (CO). The presence of protons in the solution can facilitate these electrochemical reactions. The balance between the number of protons and electrons is crucial for maintaining charge neutrality during the redox reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of scan rate in the oxidation of morphine
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 illustrates the cyclic voltammograms of morphine on a GCE surface, with scan rates varying from 10 to 300 mV/s, within a potential range of −0.2–0.7 V at a pH of 7. The data reveal that the oxidative current of morphine escalates in tandem with the scan rate. This relationship is linearly proportional to the square of the scan rate, as demonstrated in Fig. 4b. Such a correlation suggests that the oxidation process of morphine is governed by diffusion. It can be inferred that the electrochemical oxidation of morphine on the modified electrode surface occurs readily and instantaneously, upon immersion in a morphine-containing solution. To calculate the electron transfer coefficient, a plot of potential versus current was constructed at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The electron transfer coefficient was deduced to be 0.52, as indicated by the slope of Fig. 4c in conjunction with Equation 2.303 RT/ (1-α) nF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the amount of nanocomposite in electrode modification and morphine measurement
ce:section.ce:para: Figure S3 showcases the cyclic voltammograms of morphine at the electrode surface, which has been modified with varying quantities of nanocomposite. The data reveal an upward trend in the oxidative current of morphine as the nanocomposite amount is augmented from 2 to 7 μl. However, this trend plateaus and reverses when the nanocomposite quantity is further increased to 5. The observed decline in the oxidative current can be attributed to the saturation of the electrode surface with the modifier. This saturation leads to a blockage of the electrode surface, which in turn impedes the oxidation process of morphine, resulting in a diminished oxidative current.

Fig. 5a displays the differential pulse voltammogram of morphine within a potential window of 0–0.6 V at GCE/ZnS/Mil-125 surface in a pH 7 electrolyte solution. As the morphine concentration increases, the oxidative current has a corresponding rise. This relationship is linear, as depicted in Fig. 5b, where the slope of the current-concentration curve within the range of 0.01–75 is 5.0667 μA/μM. This slope value highlights the sensitivity of the method. The detection and quantification limits for morphine, calculated using Equations 3 S/m and 10 S/m, are 3 and 10 nM, respectively. Additionally, a comparison of the performance of this newly prepared electrochemical sensor with other sensors documented in the literature is provided in Table 2. The detection limits reported are on par with those achieved by this method. Moreover, the sensor developed in this study is noted for its simplicity and cost-effectiveness, which are significant advantages of this approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and selectivity of the electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The repeatability of the modified electrode was evaluated by performing five consecutive morphine measurements at pH 7 using differential pulse voltammetry. The currents recorded from these measurements were consistent, yielding a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.21 %. Furthermore, five similarly modified electrodes were used to measure morphine, achieving an RSD of 2.74 % for the current. The stability of the modified electrode, incorporating the ZnS/Mil-125 nanocomposite, was assessed over a month. During this period, morphine measurements were carried out every four days, with the electrode being stored at 4 °C between tests. After one month, the electrode exhibited only a 4 % reduction in the oxidative current of morphine, confirming its satisfactory stability (Fig S4), repeatability, and reproducibility. The electrode’s performance was also tested with various interfering substances such as copper sulfate, sodium oxalate, caffeine, Hydrogen peroxide, ascorbic acid, dopamine, iron chloride, citric acid. The oxidative current of morphine varied by less than 5 % in these tests, demonstrating the electrode’s excellent selectivity for morphine detection under optimal conditions.

The measurement of morphine in a blood sample was conducted using an electrode modified with ZnS/Mil-125, employing the standard addition method. The outcomes are detailed in Table S1. The recovery percentages for morphine at concentrations of 0.6, 1.00, and 10.00 μM were found to be 103.33 %, 99 %, and 99.40 %, respectively. Additionally, the RSD values associated with three measurements of the real sample by the modified electrode at these concentrations were 3.36 %, 3.21 %, and 3.27 %, respectively. These results demonstrate the successful application of the modified electrode for measuring morphine in a real sample, indicating its effectiveness and reliability.

The electrochemical sensor based on the ZnS/MIL-125 nanocomposite exhibits exceptional performance due to several key factors: The nanocomposite structure provides a significantly increased surface area, which enhances the interaction between the sensor and the target analytes. This leads to improved sensitivity and faster response times. The combination of ZnS and MIL-125 can create synergistic effects that enhance electron transfer processes. This can improve the overall electrochemical activity of the sensor. MIL-125, a metal-organic framework (MOF), offers excellent stability and durability, which is crucial for long-term sensor applications. The inclusion of ZnS can further enhance these properties. ZnS contributes to improved electrical conductivity, facilitating better charge transfer during electrochemical reactions. These factors combine to provide the ZnS/MIL-125-based electrochemical sensor with exceptional sensitivity, selectivity, and stability, making it highly effective for various analytical applications. The detection limit of the sensor, found to be 3 nM, is competitive with other sensors documented in the literature. Additionally, the modifiers used in this sensor are both easy and inexpensive to synthesize, offering a considerable advantage. The concentration range tested in this study spanned from 0.01 to 75 μM. The electrode demonstrated exceptional capability in measuring morphine in real samples, affirming the suitability of this developed sensor for practical applications.
Zakyeh Akrami: Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Esmail Sohouli: Writing – original draft, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.178042.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrocatalytic determination of morphine at the surface of a carbon paste electrode spiked with a hydroquinone derivative and carbon nanotubes
J. Electroanal. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The disposition of morphine and its 3-and 6-glucuronide metabolites in humans and animals, and the importance of the metabolites to the pharmacological effects of morphine
